summaryTemplate: {{className}} is the {{patternRole}}. It wraps an object that already satisfies the same interface and adds behavior around the forwarded call. Pattern body lives at {{file}}:{{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
chosenRationaleTemplate: Tagged as Decorator because {{className}} both *holds* an instance of the component interface AND *implements* the same interface itself. Each operation forwards into the wrapped component while leaving room to layer behavior before or after the inner call. That recursive shape — a component holding a component — is the Decorator structural fingerprint.

decorator_class.note: Decorator class boundary — by satisfying the same interface as the thing it wraps, decorators can stack: `A(B(C))` is callable wherever a plain component is expected.

decorator_wrapped_component.note: Wrapped component — held by the same interface type, not a concrete one. That is what lets a decorator wrap *any* implementation, including another decorator.

decorator_forwarding_op.note: Decorated operation — the same signature as the wrapped component's operation. The decorator typically wraps the inner call with extra behavior (logging, caching, validation, formatting) without changing what the inner call does.

decorator_forwarded_call.note: Inner forward — delegates to the wrapped component. If the lines around this call are empty, the decorator is acting as a pass-through; if they contain logic, that logic is the *added* behavior the decorator contributes.

runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Adapter: Adapter wraps a class with a *different* interface to make it compatible. {{className}} wraps a class with the *same* interface to add behavior — same shape from the outside, expanded behavior on the inside.
runnerUpComparisonTemplates.Proxy: Proxy also wraps the same interface, but its job is access control or substitution (lazy load, remote call, permission gate). Decorator's job is behavior augmentation; the difference is intent visible at lines {{lineStart}}-{{lineEnd}}.
